name: Python
summary: High-level dynamically-typed language whose import system is the clearest mainstream example of modules-as-runtime-objects: import x executes x's top-level code if not yet imported, caches the resulting module object in sys.modules, and binds names; finder/loader/path-hook machinery makes import behavior environment-dependent.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Python is a dynamically-typed multi-paradigm language with the most flexible (and most loader-shaped) import system among mainstream languages. CPython is the reference implementation; alternative implementations include PyPy, Pyston, GraalPy. Compiler/runtime aspects live in `data/cpython-bytecode`, `data/cinder`, `data/numba`, `data/pyston-pyjion`, `data/python-memory-tiering`. This record covers the language-level module-system surface.

sections.title: Modules as runtime objects
sections.content:
Python's import system is the clearest mainstream example of modules as **runtime objects**. Importing performs two conceptually distinct operations — finding/loading a module and then binding it into the importing scope — but the visible effect for programmers is that `import x` executes code if `x` has not been imported yet. A Python module is therefore not just a namespace in the abstract sense; it is a cached object produced by executing top-level code.

This model extends naturally to **packages**. A regular package is typically a directory containing an `__init__.py` file, and importing the package executes that file. The package can define names, mutate `__path__`, perform setup work, or trigger further imports. Python also supports **namespace packages** (PEP 420), where `__init__.py` is absent and the import machinery synthesizes package structure from the search path. This is elegant in distribution terms, but it makes identity and resolution even more loader-sensitive.

sections.title: Finder / loader / importlib machinery
sections.content:
The import machinery itself is layered: fully qualified names, finder/loader protocols, `importlib`, path hooks, and module specs all participate. This gives Python enormous flexibility — embedders, zip imports, plugin systems, and custom importers are all viable — but it also means that "what does this import mean?" is partly answered by the runtime environment. A source file alone is not always enough.

`importlib.reload(module)` re-executes a module's top-level code in place, mutating the existing module object so all callers transparently see new bindings (with caveats about already-bound references). It is the foundation for plugin-reload patterns, but production hot-reload typically prefers process recycling because reload's correctness depends on which references downstream code has cached.

sections.title: Cycles and partial initialization
sections.content:
Cyclic imports illustrate the cost of Python's loader-centric design. Since modules are created and cached before their execution finishes, one module can observe another in a **partially initialized** state. This is not merely an annoying edge case; it is an unavoidable consequence of executable imports. The programmer must understand not just the dependency graph, but the evaluation order of top-level code and when specific names become available.

Python is the strongest case against casually allowing import-time execution. The model is powerful, but it moves initialization order, loader behavior, and side effects into what would otherwise be a simple structural feature. That cost propagates into tooling, testing, and architecture.
